turns.text: Hello Hedwig, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s strange to be on this side of the desk. Usually, I’m the one looking at the spreadsheets or drafting the policy memos about maternal outcomes. Now, I’m the one staring at the ceiling in my apartment in Stoughton, trying to figure out how to be a "good patient" when I’m barely keeping my head above water.

It feels like a massive irony. I’ve spent my career fighting for health equity, and now that I’m the one who needs the system to show up, I feel like a ghost. I’m twenty-four, I’m doing this completely on my own, and the constant stress of my blood pressure creeping up—I *know* what that means, I know the protocols—it just makes the silence in my apartment louder. 

My car died last month, and honestly? That’s my biggest hurdle right now. It sounds so small compared to the medical risks, but it’s the wall I can't climb. How do I get to my screenings? How do I get to the pharmacy? I feel like I’m performing for my midwife, nodding and saying "yes, I understand," while my stomach is in knots because I have no idea how I’m going to physically get to my next appointment. I just want someone to look at me and see me, not just the "medium risk" label on my chart.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

It’s a complicated dynamic. My midwife is kind, truly, but there’s this invisible barrier between us. When I walk into that exam room, I have to make a choice: do I show up as the public health professional who understands the terminology, or do I show up as the scared, broke woman who is terrified she’s going to have a stroke before her third trimester? Usually, I choose the former because it’s safer. If I play the part of the informed patient, maybe they won't look at me with pity.

I desperately want to be honest, though. I want to tell her that every appointment feels like a gamble because of the transportation, but I’m afraid that if I admit how unstable my situation is, I’ll just get flagged as "non-compliant." I don't want to be a statistic. I want to be a mother. 

The most important relationship is the one I *wish* I had—someone who sees the reality behind the chart. I need more than a clinical evaluation of my blood pressure; I need someone to help me map out how to get to the clinic when the bus doesn't run where I need it to. Sometimes I feel like I'm screaming for help in a language they’re not listening for.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

That’s a hard question to answer without feeling that hollow ache in my chest. To be honest, there isn't really a "support person" in that sense. I’m doing this alone. When I sit in the waiting room and see other women with their partners—holding hands, whispering about baby names, or just someone there to hold the coat while they’re being examined—I have to turn away. It’s not that I’m jealous, exactly, but it’s a sharp reminder of how heavy the silence is at home.

There’s no one to lean on when the dizziness hits, and no one to process the "what-ifs" with after I’ve read one too many articles about hypertensive disorders. It forces me to be hyper-vigilant. I don’t have the luxury of letting my guard down or being tired because I’m the only one looking out for us. It’s just me and the baby. 

Sometimes, I wonder if the clinic staff notices the empty chair next to me. They don’t say anything, and I’m grateful for that, but it highlights that I’m essentially a solo pilot navigating a storm. It shapes everything—every decision, every appointment, every sleepless night—because I’m the only one holding the map. I’m just trying to make sure we make it to the other side safely.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

I suppose when you’re navigating this path entirely on your own, the "relationship" I’m really forced to grapple with is the one I have with myself. There isn’t a partner here to create tension with, but the absence of one definitely creates a different kind of friction. 

I find myself getting angry at myself—which is irrational, I know—for not having a better plan, or for not having a partner to share this burden with. When I get home after a stressful appointment where my blood pressure was borderline high, there’s nobody to talk it out with, no one to tell me it’s going to be okay. So, the tension is internal. It’s a constant tug-of-war between the part of me that knows the medical data and the part of me that’s just a tired woman who feels abandoned by the circumstances of her life.

I think about the future a lot. I wonder if I’ll resent this silence later, or if I’ll look back and be proud of how I handled the isolation. It’s lonely, truly. It changes how you view the world when you realize that, in the most vulnerable moment of your life, the only hand you have to hold is your own. I’m doing my best to be a good mother already, but god, it’s heavy.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

Honestly? I don’t turn to people. Not really. I’ve spent my whole professional life being the "helper," the person people come to when they need to navigate systems or find resources. Admitting that I’m the one who needs help—that I’m struggling to get to the doctor or that I’m terrified of my own body—feels like a failure. It feels like I’m failing the very advocacy work I used to do.

I have a few acquaintances, but they have their own lives, their own partners, their own stability. If I reach out, I’m just "the girl with the problems." So, I mostly turn to the internet, which is a dangerous trap. I scroll through forums at 3:00 a.m., trying to find stories that sound like mine, looking for some kind of validation that I’m not going to fall through the cracks. But reading those posts just makes the "medium risk" label feel like a neon sign flashing over my head.

It creates this weird disconnect. My formal care is all about clinical checklists and blood pressure cuffs, while my "informal support" is just me, late at night, spiraling through search results. None of it replaces the human presence I’m missing. I need someone who isn't a medical professional and isn't a stranger on a screen. I need a real, living, breathing person who doesn't need me to be the "good patient." But I haven't found that yet.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

It’s funny, the people I end up leaning on aren't the ones I expected. I’ve actually found a strange, quiet connection with the woman who drives the community shuttle sometimes. She doesn’t know I’m pregnant, or at least she hasn't asked, but she’s the only one who doesn't look at me like a "case." She’s just a person who notices when I’m struggling to get on the bus with my bag, or when I look particularly pale. 

There’s also a librarian at the branch in Stoughton. I started going there because it has decent Wi-Fi and it’s a quiet place to sit when my apartment feels too suffocating. She’s seen me there repeatedly, usually with my books and my laptop, and she’s started leaving little notes or just checking in with a nod. It’s not "support" in the way a partner or a family member would be, but it’s a tiny thread of recognition. 

It’s ironic—I’m surrounded by a medical system that is supposed to be "supporting" me, but I find more humanity in the people who have zero obligation to care. It makes me realize that I’m starving for that kind of casual, low-stakes human contact. I didn't think I'd be leaning on strangers in public spaces, but they’re the only ones who don't expect me to be a "good patient." They just see me as a person. It’s not a substitute for a support system, but it’s the only thing keeping me steady right now.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

I had to make a decision about extra monitoring recently. My blood pressure had hit a point where the midwife suggested more frequent scans and a few additional tests to rule out preeclampsia concerns. 

In that exam room, it felt like an interrogation. She was looking at me, waiting for a "yes," and I felt that familiar pressure to just agree because that’s what the "good patient" does. But my mind was racing with the logistics: *How? How do I get here three times a week? What does that do to my job? Can I afford the rideshare on top of everything else?*

I didn't have anyone to bounce that off of. I was standing there in that thin, scratchy gown, literally holding my breath. I wanted to ask, "If I can't get here, what happens to the baby?" but I was terrified that if I admitted I couldn't make the appointments, they’d label me as "non-compliant" or, worse, call social services on me. 

So, I made the decision alone. I said "yes" because the risk to the baby felt bigger than the risk of losing my job or emptying my savings. I walked out of that clinic feeling like I’d just signed a contract I didn't know how to fulfill. I carried that entire weight by myself, and it’s been burning a hole in my stomach ever since.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

My employer is a source of constant, low-level panic. I work full-time, and I’m terrified that every time I have to leave for an appointment—especially these new, frequent ones—I’m proving I’m unreliable. I haven't told them I’m pregnant yet. I’m waiting for the "right time," but the longer I wait, the more I feel like I’m hiding a secret that’s already starting to show in my lethargy and my physical discomfort. I’m sure they’ve noticed I’m not the same high-performing person I was six months ago. The pressure to keep my head down and just "power through" is immense because I know I need this income to survive, and if I lose this job, I lose everything.

My community in Stoughton feels like a distant neighbor. I moved here for the affordability, not for the village. There’s no faith community or cultural circle holding me up. I’ve always been fiercely independent, the type of woman who handles things herself, but this pregnancy has stripped away that shield. 

The pressure is almost entirely self-inflicted, I think. I feel like I’m constantly failing at being the woman I told myself I would be. I thought I’d have a partner, a savings account, a house—not this quiet, solitary race against a blood pressure cuff and a bus schedule. It’s a lonely kind of pressure, and I’m carrying it all behind a smile that feels thinner every day.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

It’s a constant, jarring collision. I spent years building an identity around being "capable"—the advocate, the one who solves the problems, the person who holds the institutional knowledge. Now, I look in the mirror and I see someone who is dependent on the very systems I used to critique. 

There’s this deep, jagged tension between "Public Health Hedwig" and "Patient Hedwig." The professional side of me looks at my chart and sees a person who needs stability, transport, and nutrition support. But the actual me? I just feel like I’m losing my footing. I feel like a hypocrite. How can I be an expert on equity when I can’t even secure my own access to basic prenatal care? 

I feel less like a person and more like a series of data points that aren't aligning correctly. It’s like I’m watching myself from the ceiling—seeing this young woman, stressed, exhausted, and alone, while simultaneously analyzing her risks as if I’m reading a case study for work. It’s a dissociative way to experience your own life. I’m mourning the woman I was—the independent, bulletproof version of myself—and I’m not yet the woman I need to be to survive this. It’s a hard, lonely place to inhabit.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

It’s the vulnerability that gets me. I’m an adult woman, I have a career, I have a brain, but when I’m in that stirrup position, I’m just a body. A "medium risk" body. 

Most of the time, it’s just me and the midwife. And honestly, that silence is heavy. When she’s measuring my fundal height or pressing down on my abdomen to check the baby’s position, I’m hyper-aware of how much of my internal life is suddenly public record. She’s looking at my vitals, but she isn't looking at *me*. 

I find myself holding my breath, not because of the physical discomfort, but because of the emotional exposure. I’m thinking, *Does she know I’m hungry? Does she know I’m scared I won’t be able to get back here next week?* I feel like I’m in a fishbowl. Every time she notes something down, I’m bracing myself to see if I’m "passing" or "failing." 

If I had someone else there—a friend, a partner, anyone—I think it would change the power dynamic. It would anchor me to the world outside those four walls. But alone? It feels like the power is entirely in her pen. She dictates the reality of my pregnancy, and I just have to nod and hope the story she’s writing ends with a healthy baby, regardless of how much I’m falling apart in the process.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

It makes a world of difference, even if I don’t say it out loud. When I sit in that exam room alone, the power dynamic is suffocating. I’m the one in the gown, the one whose blood pressure is being dissected, the one whose life is being labeled "medium risk." Without someone there to act as a buffer, I’m completely defenseless. 

I’ve had moments—like during my last scan—where I felt so exposed I almost couldn't speak. You’re lying there, vulnerable, and the technician is just looking at a screen, talking about the baby like it’s a science project. I desperately wanted to reach out and hold someone’s hand, just to prove I wasn't just a clinical case study. When you’re alone, you have to be the advocate for yourself, the patient, the terrified mother, *and* the logistics manager all at once. It’s too much.

If I had someone with me—even just for the long, nerve-wracking moments before the heartbeat starts—I think I could breathe. That person would be a witness to my reality, not just the data on the chart. They would be someone who knows that my blood pressure is high because I’m stressed about my life, not just because my body is failing. Being alone feels like being stripped of your humanity in front of people who are just doing their job. It’s profoundly isolating.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

Honestly? The "navigating" part is all me, and that's exactly why it feels like I’m barely keeping my head above water. I don’t have a mentor or a partner to help decode the bureaucracy. When I’m at the clinic, I’m reading the paperwork and translating the clinical jargon for myself, because that’s what I’ve been trained to do professionally. But there’s a brutal difference between knowing how a system works on paper and actually *being* the person trying to fight through the bureaucracy when you’re exhausted and alone.

The hardest part is that I don’t have an advocate. If I’m confused about a policy or if I need to push back against a scheduling conflict that doesn't work with my transit situation, there’s no one to stand in my corner. The administrative staff, they’re just following protocol. They don’t see that I’m trying to bridge a gap between my knowledge and my physical reality—they just see a name on a schedule that’s asking for an exception. 

I’m the one booking the rides, reading the pamphlets, and triple-checking my insurance coverage. Every time I hit a wall—like when a receptionist tells me they can’t change a time, regardless of the bus—I feel that wall between me and a healthy birth getting higher. I don't have anyone to lean on to make this easier, and the burden of carrying all that "navigation" feels like it’s stealing the time I should be spending just… being pregnant. Just being a mother.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When I first found out, I think I held onto this naive, idealized version of what care would look like. I pictured a mentor, maybe an older clinician I’d worked with, someone who would just *know*—someone who would look at my chart and say, "Hedwig, I’ve got you, we’re going to figure out the logistics together." 

I didn't have anyone like that in my actual life, but I think I spent the first few weeks *searching* for that person in every appointment. I went in wanting to trust the system because I’d spent my career believing in it. I thought, "Well, I know the protocols, so the providers will see me as an equal." But that trust shattered pretty quickly. 

Trust is a two-way street, right? And I realize now that I haven't found anyone who I can truly rely on because none of them know the *whole* story. How can I trust a provider who doesn't know that my car is dead, or that I’m eating on a budget that’s being squeezed dry by these appointments, or that my blood pressure is spiking partly because I’m paralyzed with fear about being a single mom? 

I’m still waiting for someone to ask the right questions—not the ones on the intake form, but the ones that actually touch on the reality of being a woman walking this path alone. Until then, I’m just performing trust. It’s safer that way.
Were there any moments where that trust felt shaken, or where you felt your concerns weren't being taken seriously?

It’s the small, dismissive moments that hurt the most. Like when I mentioned my transportation struggle—just a passing comment, trying to explain why I was five minutes late and breathless—and the provider just jotted it down without looking up. She didn't ask, "How are you getting here?" or "Is there anything we can do to make this easier?" She just moved on to the next item on the checklist. 

In that moment, I felt like a line item, not a person. It’s infuriating because I know the system *could* connect me to a voucher or a community resource, but instead, I’m treated like I’m just perpetually disorganized. That’s when the trust breaks. I realize that I’m not being "cared for"—I’m being processed. 

The worst part is that I have to swallow my pride and keep going back. I need the ultrasounds to see the baby is healthy; I need the midwife to keep monitoring that blood pressure. I feel like I’m trapped in a system that assumes every patient has a stable car, a supportive partner, and a calm life. When I don’t have those things, the system doesn’t adjust; it just treats me like I’m the one who is failing. That isn't care. It’s just bureaucracy disguised as medicine.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Before I was pregnant, I worked in clinics as a researcher. I saw how we spoke to patients—the shorthand, the paternalism, the way we’d explain a procedure as if we were presenting a fixed set of facts rather than an invitation to collaborate. I thought I was better than that. I thought I knew how to "partner" with patients. 

But now that I’m the one in the gown, the irony is crushing. I see the same patterns happening to me, and it's infuriating. When a doctor says, "We need to schedule this test for Tuesday at 9:00 AM," it’s framed as an objective necessity, not a negotiable choice. If I speak up and mention my work schedule or my transportation, the tone shifts immediately. They get this tight, clipped patience—like I’m being "difficult" or "non-compliant"—instead of acknowledging that their scheduling doesn't account for a patient's entire life.

It feels like my agency is being eroded in fifteen-minute increments. They’re managing the "risk," but they aren’t managing *me*. I feel like I’m being pushed through a conveyor belt where the decision-making is already done before I even open my mouth. It’s a total loss of power, and it’s exhausting to have to fight just to be treated like an adult who manages her own life.
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

It was during my third or fourth prenatal visit. I’d just been flagged again for the elevated blood pressure, and the midwife, who is usually very professional, essentially dropped a new, more rigorous monitoring plan on my lap. She said, "We’re going to need you in here twice a week now, at least. We’ll do a non-stress test and a follow-up assessment."

It was just her and me. I remember looking at the clock on the wall—it was ticking so loudly—and thinking about my shift at work and the fact that my car wasn't starting. I tried to speak up. I said, "I’m not sure how I can manage that with my transit situation. Can we look at a later time or, I don't know, a telehealth check-in for some of these?"

The air in the room just… shifted. She didn't look angry, but she looked *impatient*. She gave me that tight-lipped, professional smile that says, "I am the expert here, and you are the one putting your baby at risk." She told me, "Hedwig, these tests are non-negotiable given your readings."

I felt so small. I felt like I was being scolded, like a child who wasn't doing her homework. I didn't say another word. I just nodded and took the paper. I felt like I was drowning, and I was so ashamed of my own life that I just let her dictate the terms. It was the most isolating, powerless moment of this entire pregnancy. I walked out of that clinic feeling like I’d failed before I even got to the bus stop.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

Before this, I felt like my body was a tool—an engine that I maintained so I could do my work, hit my goals, and navigate the world on my own terms. I never really romanticized pregnancy. I saw it through a lens of epidemiology: I knew the biology, I knew the risks of preeclampsia, and I knew the statistics of maternal morbidity. I looked at it as a physiological hurdle to clear, like a high-stakes professional project.

I didn't talk about it openly with anyone, mostly because I was always the "independent one." I didn't want to talk about "someday" or "family planning" because I was so busy trying to build a career that could support a future. In my head, I figured I’d be prepared—that I’d have the car, the safety net, the partner—before I ever crossed that threshold.

I feel like a fool now. I thought I could out-think the risk by being educated, but I couldn't out-think the reality of my circumstances. I look at my body now, and it doesn't feel like a tool anymore. It feels like a vessel that’s betraying me—bloating, spiking in blood pressure, shaking when I’m nervous. It’s no longer *my* body; it’s a clinical entity under surveillance. I’ve lost that sense of ownership I once had, and that loss is just as heavy as the isolation.
Were there parts of your relationship with your body that felt complicated or uncertain as you thought about becoming pregnant?

The complication wasn't just the biological stuff I studied; it was the fear that my body, which I’ve worked so hard to keep independent and functional, was suddenly going to become a "liability" in the eyes of others. 

I’ve always been the type of woman who prides herself on not needing help, on walking fast, working hard, and being physically reliable. The thought of pregnancy—even before it happened—felt like a surrender of that autonomy. I worried about how it would look to others, especially at work. Would I be seen as "less than"? Would my physical state be used against me, or would I be treated like I’m fragile? 

Now that I’m here, it’s not just a worry; it’s an active tension. I’m constantly trying to suppress the physical symptoms of the stress—the racing heart, the dizziness—so I can "pass" as the version of myself that doesn't need support. It’s like I’m fighting my own physiology because I know that if my body shows the slightest bit of vulnerability, the "medium risk" label will become the only thing anyone sees. It’s a strange, bitter irony: I spent my whole life building a body that could take on the world, and now, I’m terrified that this same body is what’s going to make me a statistic.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

Before I was pregnant, I relied on peer-reviewed literature, public health data, and professional repositories. I trusted the *system* because I thought the system was neutral—objective data points that couldn't be biased. I had this professional hubris that I could filter out the noise.

But now? That trust in the "reliable" sources feels hollow. I can read the ACOG guidelines on hypertension until my eyes burn, but those guidelines don't account for the fact that I’m sitting on a bus that’s breaking down while I try to get to the lab. The "reliable" sources are written for a patient profile that assumes a baseline level of stability—a partner, a reliable car, a flexible job, a support system. 

When I search online now, I actually find myself gravitating toward the forums, the messy, human, anecdotal threads. I’m looking for the "how-to" of survival, not the clinical protocol. I’ve realized that the sources I once valued for their accuracy are actually missing the most critical data: the *how.* How do you survive the medical system when you’re poor? How do you keep your blood pressure down when you’re terrified? 

It’s been a harsh lesson in humility. The clinical data is accurate, sure, but it’s incomplete. It tells me what *should* happen to a healthy body, not how to keep a woman like me from falling through the cracks.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

I was always hyper-aware of the numbers, even before I was pregnant. Maybe it’s just the professional in me, but I knew that my age—being young, being single—and my lack of a documented "support system" were all variables that clinics track. It’s like being a case study waiting to happen.

I think there’s this unspoken judgment that follows women who don’t fit the "ideal" demographic. Even before I had a positive test, I felt like I was already being graded. You walk into a clinic, and you can almost feel the provider sizing you up, categorizing you based on your zip code, your job, your marital status. I’ve always felt like I had to be twice as articulate, twice as organized, and twice as "composed" just to be treated with the same basic respect that someone with a wedding ring or a more affluent background gets automatically.

It feels like living in a glass house where everyone is looking for the cracks in your foundation. I wasn't wrong, either. Now that I’m actually pregnant, that "medium risk" label feels like the system’s way of saying, *“We knew you’d be a problem.”* It makes you want to hide, to shrink, to make yourself as small as possible so they don't have a reason to flag you further. It’s a weight I’ve been carrying for a long time.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

Oh, all the time. It’s almost like they have a specialized "tone" they use for people like me—a mixture of clinical pity and subtle frustration, like I’m a puzzle piece that refuses to fit into their pre-arranged board.

I remember an intake appointment where the person entering my data kept pausing. When she got to "marital status," she just blinked at me and clicked, and then she did this visible exhale before typing in "never married." It wasn’t a question; it was a judgment. I felt the air leave the room. It was like she’d already tagged me as "at-risk" before she even asked how I was feeling. 

It’s that constant, nagging feeling that I’m being sized up for my deficits rather than my strengths. They look at my history, my background, my lack of a partner, and they see a problem to be managed. They don’t see the woman who finished her degree, who’s holding down a full-time job, who’s trying to navigate a broken healthcare system in a broken car. They see the "medium risk" in red ink. It makes me feel so small, so exposed. I’m not just a woman having a baby; I’m a collection of vulnerabilities that they’re just waiting to see fail. It makes me want to scream, but instead, I just sit there, nodding. It's the only way to get through the appointment without being labeled "difficult."
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

There wasn’t a partner in the room when I decided I was ready. That’s the part I struggle to say out loud because it’s not just a fact; it’s a failure, or at least that’s what society screams at you. When I started thinking about motherhood, I realized I was going to be the only person in the room for everything. 

Back then, I didn't think it would be such a heavy, crushing weight. I thought, "I’m educated, I know the systems, I can handle the logistics." I looked at it as a solo mission—a project where I could be the one in control. I didn’t talk to friends or family about it because I knew the skepticism I’d face. People want to ask, "Do you have a partner? Is this the right time? Can you afford it?" and I wasn't looking for a debate. I was looking for a future. 

Now, when I go to those prenatal appointments, I feel that void in the room. I see other women with their partners, and it isn't just about someone holding a hand; it’s about someone being a witness to the process. I’m the only one who knows the truth of how tired I am or how much I’m stressing over the rent. Not having that shared history with someone else makes the clinical decisions feel so much colder, so much more final. I’m making choices for two people, but I’m the only one bearing the weight of the consequences.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

There is no partner to bring. I go alone, every single time. 

I’ve spent a lot of time imagining what it would be like if someone *was* there. I think about the dynamic shifting instantly—the way the midwife’s gaze might soften, the way they might actually explain things more thoroughly rather than just rattling off instructions to "the patient." When you’re alone, you’re just a target for their advice; when you have a partner, you’re suddenly a couple, a unit, a little family. There’s a legitimacy to your existence that I just don't have.

I see those other women in the waiting room, and their partners are the ones who ask the questions I’m too tired or intimidated to ask. They’re the ones who offer the water, the ones who help them up from the table. Without that person, I feel like I’m fighting a battle on two fronts: one against my own body and these rising blood pressure numbers, and the other against the loneliness of the clinic. It forces me to be the "good patient" even harder, because if I break down or admit how much I’m struggling, there’s no one there to hold the pieces. It’s just me and the flickering fluorescent lights of the exam room. It is a very, very quiet experience.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

I worked in health equity, so I’ve been on both sides of that fence. Before this, when I was working as a researcher, I actually felt like my voice had weight. My input was valued in meetings, and people would listen when I pointed out systemic gaps. I felt like I was part of the solution, and that made me feel safe. It gave me this dangerous sense of security—the idea that if you know how the system works, you’re somehow immune to being treated like a number.

But the moment I walked into my first prenatal screening as a *patient*, that weight evaporated. My voice didn't matter because I wasn't there as a professional colleague; I was a data point to be managed. 

I remember trying to tell a provider about my fatigue, and how it was affecting my work. She didn't listen; she just wrote "complains of fatigue" in my chart as if it were a minor, expected symptom rather than a life-altering exhaustion. That was the first time I realized that even if I know all the terminology, I’m still just a "compliant" or "non-compliant" vessel to them. I’m treated like an object that needs to be monitored, not a person with a life that’s actually falling apart. It’s a total shift from being the one with the microphone to being the one they’re talking *at* instead of *with*. It’s demoralizing.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

In the beginning, I wanted to be a collaborator. I wanted the midwife to see me as a partner, someone who brought my clinical knowledge and my life experience to the table, and for us to map out a path together. I thought that if I was educated and reasonable, the power dynamic would be horizontal. 

But as the weeks dragged on and my blood pressure numbers kept climbing, I realized that I wasn't being invited to a discussion. I was being given directives. My "wishes"—like wanting to space out appointments to accommodate my transit reality, or wanting to explore non-pharmaceutical ways to lower my stress—were consistently brushed aside. 

Now, my goal has shifted from "collaboration" to "survival." I don’t care if they respect my wishes anymore, as long as they don't lose the baby. I feel like I’ve had to trade my autonomy for safety. I’ve become the "good patient" who nods and checks the boxes, even when I’m screaming inside, because I’m terrified that if I push back, they’ll see me as "difficult." It’s a bitter, hollow feeling. I’m in charge of *nothing*—not the appointment times, not the tests, not even the way my own body is behaving. I’m just waiting for the day I can walk out of that clinic and finally, finally have my life back.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

It’s just me. Every single time I have to make a choice—whether it's about extra scans, medication options, or how to juggle work and these increasingly frequent appointments—there isn't an "us" to talk it over with. It’s just me and my own anxiety, weighing my bank account against my blood pressure. 

Sometimes, I find myself wishing I could outsource the decision-making, just to have a break from the constant, low-level panic. I see other women with partners who show up with notebooks, who ask the questions I'm too drained to ask, who advocate for them when they feel invisible. It makes my heart ache, honestly. When you don't have that, you have to be your own advocate, your own logistics coordinator, and your own emotional support system. It’s exhausting. 

I’ve had to cultivate this exterior, this "good patient" mask, because I don’t have a partner to vent to at home. I have to save all my energy for the clinic. It feels incredibly lopsided. I’m the one carrying the pregnancy, the one managing the logistics of surviving it, and the one holding the fear of what happens if I can’t get to the next appointment. I’m doing this entire, massive life event in a vacuum. It’s not just a lack of help—it’s a lack of a witness to the struggle.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

It’s like you’re asking me about a parallel universe. I don’t have a partner to bring, and honestly, the silence where that person should be is the loudest part of every appointment. 

I imagine that if I *did* have someone with me, the midwife might actually look up from her chart more often. They might slow down their explanation of my lab results instead of rushing through them like they’re reciting a shopping list. There’s a certain social currency to having a partner—it signals that you’re "stable," that there’s a support system, that someone else is invested in the outcome. Without that, I feel like I have to work twice as hard to prove that I’m a responsible, "together" person, just so they don’t dismiss me or focus only on my "risks."

It’s exhausting to wonder if the care I’m getting would be different if I wasn't walking in alone. Would they have been more patient about my transportation issues? Would they have listened when I asked for a different time? When you’re solo, you’re just a single point of failure in their system. I feel like I have to perform being a "good, low-maintenance patient" to make up for the fact that I’m the only one in that room. It’s a lonely, heavy performance.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It’s been a total detachment. I used to be proud of my body’s efficiency—it was the machine that got me through my degree, my jobs, my activism. Now, I feel like a passenger in a vehicle that’s breaking down in the middle of the highway. Between the blood pressure spikes and the constant, gnawing fatigue, it feels less like "my" body and more like a high-risk site under constant surveillance.

There is no one I feel safe talking to about this. If I told the midwife, she’d just re-calibrate the "risk" score. If I told my coworkers, I’d be labeled "unreliable." If I told my friends, they’d just offer platitudes like "it’s all for the baby," which feels like a slap in the face when I’m struggling to just keep us both upright. 

So, I keep it inside. I sit with the resentment and the fear alone. I feel betrayed by my own physiology, and the worst part is the guilt. You’re supposed to feel "connected" or "glowing," but I just feel like I’m in a silent, high-stakes battle. I’m just trying to keep the "medium risk" label from becoming a permanent mark. It’s a very solitary, very lonely kind of mourning for the person I was before the paper gown.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

It’s the subtle, clinical ways they talk about me that sting the most. It’s never one big, dramatic comment, but a hundred small ones. When my midwife looks at my chart and says, “We need to watch your weight gain—your BMI is a factor,” it isn’t just a medical observation. It feels like an accusation. It’s like they’re saying my body is failing the assignment, even though I’m just trying to keep us both alive on a tight budget.

Every time they bring up my blood pressure, the tone is one of mild, clinical disappointment, as if my body is being "disobedient." It makes me feel like I’m a science project that’s not quite conforming to the data set. I’m not a woman who’s tired and scared; I’m a collection of "risks" that need to be mitigated.

Even the way they do the ultrasounds—they move the wand over my belly like they’re checking the engine of a car. They don’t look at me. They don’t see the woman who’s trying to hold it all together. They see a "medium risk" body that’s being a hassle to manage. It makes me want to cover up, to hide, to apologize for being here at all. I’ve stopped feeling like I live in my body and started feeling like I’m just renting space in a high-maintenance machine that everyone is judging.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that really gnaws at me—the thing I don't think I’ve fully articulated—is the sheer *invisibility* of the effort. 

People see a pregnant woman in a waiting room, and they think the "hard work" is the physical pregnancy itself—the nausea, the weight, the swelling. But for me, the hardest part is the invisible labor: the hours spent mapping out bus routes to avoid being late, the frantic emails to my job to cover shifts so I don't lose my income, the mental gymnastics of hiding my panic so I don't get "flagged" as a problem patient. 

I want people to understand that for women in my position—alone, working, trying to navigate these massive, impersonal systems—the "care" we receive is often just the bare minimum, while the barriers to actually *accessing* that care are massive. I feel like I’m fighting a war on two fronts: one against my own biology, and the other against the logistics of just existing in this world while trying to keep a baby safe. 

If I could ask for one thing from the system, it wouldn't be more leaflets or more "risk" warnings. It would be someone who asks, "How are you actually going to get here?" and then helps me solve it. It’s the humanizing of the logistics that would change everything. I just want my daughter to know her mom did everything she could, against all odds, to bring her here.
